title: Changing the Contrast of Magnetic Resonance Imaging Signals using Deep Learning - Supplementary Material
author: Simkó, Attila
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
Modern radiotherapy often requires MRI scans of the same region with one specific or multiple different contrasts. As each contrast highlights different tissues, automated segmentation tools might be optimized for a certain contrast, while multiple scans can achieve a better accuracy for delineating tumours and organs at risk. Unfortunately, the optimal contrast for the subsequent automated methods might not be known during the time of signal acquisition, and performing multiple scans with different contrasts increases the total examination time and registering the sequences introduces extra work and potential errors. Building on the recent achievements using Deep Learning in medical applications, the presented work describes a novel approach for transferring between any two contrasts. 

 A model was developed for spin echo sequences to transfer any contrast to any other by changing the Echo and Repetition Time (TE and TR respectively) for the given protocol. 
The novel model architecture incorporates the signal equation for spin echo sequences, and hence the model inherently learns the unknown quantitative maps for proton density, T1 and T2 relaxation times (PD, T1 and T2, respectively). This grants the model the ability to retrospectively reconstruct signals with any TE and TR combination. 

 The results show the robustness and accuracy of the model for physically consistent contrast transfer. The semi-supervised training process does not require multi-contrast scans, only scans from a wide range of contrasts. This means that the experiments are easily reproducible with other common contrasts, or for other patient anatomies. Realized in practice, the proposed method would greatly simplify the modern radiotherapy pipeline. 

   

 The trained model is available on this Zenodo page 'contrast_transfer_keras.h5' together with a tool 'slider_tool.exe' for testing the model on three example images '*_example.png' acquired with the same protocol as the data for training. For the tool to work, make sure it's in the same folder as the trained model. 

 After running 'slider_tool.exe', and loading the images --- which have to be 256x256 in order to work --- the model will decompose the signal into PD, T1 and T2. Then, you can use the sliders for TE and TR to change the contrast of the signal. 

 With any issues you encounter regarding the trained model, please contact the authors.

This grants the model the ability to retrospectively reconstruct signals with any TE and TR combination.
